original_abstract: Neural machine translation (NMT) represents a paradigm shift in statistical translation, utilizing end-to-end neural networks to map source sequences to target languages. Typically, these architectures rely on an encoder-decoder framework: the encoder compresses a variable-length input sentence into a fixed-size vector representation, which the decoder then uses to generate a translation. This paper investigates the nuanced properties of NMT by evaluating two distinct architectures: the traditional RNN Encoder-Decoder and a novel gated recursive convolutional neural network. Our analysis reveals that while NMT systems excel at translating short sentences with familiar vocabulary, their performance deteriorates significantly as sentence length increases or when encountering out-of-vocabulary words. Additionally, our findings demonstrate that the proposed gated recursive convolutional network is capable of autonomously capturing the underlying grammatical structure of sentences during the learning process.
humanized_abstract: Neural machine translation (NMT) is an innovative approach in statistical translation techniques, which use end-to-end neural networks to translate a source sentence in a particular language to a target language. Most of the NMT models are based on encoder–decoder architecture, in which the input sentence is encoded into a fixed length vector by the encoder and this vector is then fed to the decoder to produce the output sentence. In this paper, we investigated some aspects of NMT models and developed two models, namely RNN Encoder-Decoder and Gated Recursive Convolutional Network (GRCN) to observe their performance. Our experiments show that although current state of the art NMT models perform very well on short sentences with high frequency words in the target language, they show a dramatic reduction in accuracy for longer sentences and for rare words. Our GRCN model can automatically learn the sentence grammatical components while training.
